# GitHub Copilot instructions

This `.github/` directory is a **self-contained Copilot configuration bundle**: a
ticket workflow plus a couple of authoring/review helpers, written entirely as
prompt-and-instruction Markdown. Nothing here depends on any other tool's config —
it works on its own.

## The ticket workflow

A backend-agnostic, in-repo issue tracker driven by seven prompt files (invoke with
`/` in Copilot Chat). Configuration lives in `.github/config.yaml`, generated on
first use by `/ticket-init`.

| Command | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| `/ticket-init` | Bootstrap a project: write `.github/config.yaml`, create stage folders or workflow labels, lay down a starter ticket template. One-time. |
| `/ticket-new` | Create one ticket — or a small slate — through a gated flow that reconciles your intent with the agent's understanding before anything is committed. |
| `/ticket-refine` | Resume a captured inbox entry and promote it to backlog (or close as fold/wontfix). Only if an inbox stage is configured. |
| `/ticket-pick` | Pull the next ticket off the backlog and implement it through to review. |
| `/ticket-review` | Print a read-only verification guide for a ticket in review. |
| `/ticket-close` | Close a ticket as shipped, trusting you've verified the work. |
| `/grill-me` | Stress-test a plan or design by interviewing you down each branch of the decision tree. |

The shared execution layer lives in [`shared/ticket-engine.md`](shared/ticket-engine.md)
(config load/validate, ID assignment, backend transitions, commit/comment
formatting, half-state reporting) and [`shared/milestone-sync.md`](shared/milestone-sync.md)
(milestone drift detection/repair). Each `/ticket-*` prompt reads the engine and
follows its matching operation inline — there is no separate runtime.

## How this bundle is organized

```
.github/
├── copilot-instructions.md          this file (always loaded by Copilot)
├── prompts/                         slash commands — /ticket-* and /grill-me
│   ├── ticket-{init,new,refine,pick,review,close}.prompt.md
│   └── grill-me.prompt.md
├── instructions/
│   └── interface-copy.instructions.md   auto-loads on UI files (applyTo glob)
├── shared/
│   ├── ticket-engine.md             execution layer, referenced by every /ticket-*
│   └── milestone-sync.md            milestone drift sync, referenced by pick/close
├── skills/
│   └── writing-for-interfaces/      UX/interface copy guidance (referenced by instructions)
└── config.yaml                      generated by /ticket-init (not shipped)
```

## Conventions used throughout

- **User gates are numbered lists.** Where a prompt needs a discrete choice, it
  prints the options as `N. **Label** — description` and you reply with the number.
  Never silently pick an option that changes scope, type, acceptance criteria, or
  size. Free-text follow-ups stay as plain questions.
- **Engine delegation is by reference.** When a prompt says "follow
  `shared/ticket-engine.md`", read that file and execute the matching operation
  inline with your file and terminal tools. **No user gates live in the engine** —
  the calling prompt owns every gate.
- **Arguments** come through prompt input variables (e.g. `${input:ticketId}`) or
  the text typed after the command.

## Hard rules (every ticket transition)

- **Never amend** an existing commit. Every workflow event is a new commit
  (filesystem) or a new API call (GitHub).
- **Never `--no-verify`.** Never bypass commit signing.
- **`git mv` before frontmatter edit** on rename+edit transitions, so
  `git log --follow` survives.
- **One workflow event = one commit** (filesystem) / one issue mutation (GitHub).
- **Stop and report on partial failure.** Never auto-rollback; surface the
  half-state precisely.
- **Never re-issue an ID.** Dropped IDs stay reserved as gaps.
- **Tickets are the source of truth.** Milestone trackers and summaries reflect
  tickets, never the reverse.
- **GitHub Project sync is best-effort** and never authoritative (github backend
  only).

## Getting started

1. Run `/ticket-init` and answer the numbered prompts. It generates
   `.github/config.yaml` tailored to your backend (filesystem or GitHub).
2. Capture your first piece of work with `/ticket-new`.
3. Implement it with `/ticket-pick`, then close it out with `/ticket-close`.
